experiments.title: heuristic tool selector (keyword-based top-3)
experiments.description:
# experiment: heuristic tool selector (keyword-based top-3)

## Hypothesis

Adding a deterministic, lightweight keyword-based tool selector that restricts the agent to the top-3 most relevant tools per task will (1) increase reliably passing cases by reducing tool confusion/noise and (2) reduce average passing-case time by avoiding unnecessary tool invocations. This targets both levers: increase passing cases and reduce mean passing-case time.

## Motivation

The repo's ideas emphasize a small harness and tool-selection as high-impact, low-cost changes. A full ML-based selector is heavy; a simple keyword-overlap heuristic is cheap to implement (<50 LOC), deterministic, and likely to avoid worst-case tool misuses that harm correctness and latency.

## Type

Prompting / tooling experiment (agent prompt + lightweight helper)

## Mutation surface

- agents/edge.py (single-file change)

## Implementation Instructions

1. Add a small helper near prompt-build logic in agents/edge.py (<= ~40 LOC):
   - def select_tools_by_keywords(prompt: str, tools: list[dict], k: int = 3) -> list[dict]:
       - Tokenize prompt and each tool's name/short description (split on non-word chars, lowercased).
       - Score each tool by keyword overlap count (intersection size).
       - Tie-break deterministically by tool name.
       - Return top-k tools (or all tools if all scores are zero).
2. Integrate the helper into the prompt construction path:
   - Before serializing available tools into the agent prompt, call select_tools_by_keywords(user_prompt, tools, k=3).
   - Only include the returned tools' descriptions in the prompt (keep the same format as existing tool docs so downstream parsing is unchanged).
3. Add a small config constant near the top of agents/edge.py: TOOL_SELECTOR_K = 3 to make tuning easy.
4. Keep behaviour non-destructive: when debug mode or an env var (EDGE_AGENT_TOOL_SELECTOR=off) is set, fall back to including all tools.

Notes on implementation style:
- Keep changes minimal and local to agents/edge.py (no new deps).
- Use plain Python sets and sorted() for deterministic ties.
- Add unit-friendly separation: keep select_tools_by_keywords as a pure function so tests can call it.

## Validation(code, prompts, tasks)

- Run the agent smoke prompts before/after the change:
  - just edge-agent "What is the age the 3rd president of the US when he took office?"
  - just edge-agent "Summarize the last 24 hours system logs and flag anomalies"
- Run the evals runner for the edge agent baseline set and compare metrics:
  - just eval "edge_agent_default"  (compare passing_cases and avg_passing_case_seconds against baseline)
- Inspect traces/logs to confirm:
  - Only up to 3 tools are included in the prompt per task.
  - Tool invocation counts decrease or stay same; correctness does not regress.

Success criteria:
- No regressions in passing cases (ideally +1 or more)
- Average passing-case seconds reduced by >=5% on passing cases

## Anticipated missteps and fallbacks

- Selector returns zero overlap for many tasks, causing fallback to all tools — acceptable; if it happens often, change k to 5 or augment scoring with simple tf-idf weights.
- Selector omits a crucial but low-overlap tool (e.g., diagnostics tool invoked by generic prompts). Fallback: expose EDGE_AGENT_TOOL_SELECTOR_K env var and allow per-task overrides in the prompt metadata.
- If deterministic ties cause pathological ordering, add a short priority map for essential tools (configurable list) to ensure critical tools are retained.

## Sources

- /workspace/docs/ideas.md — suggested Harness + Model Routing and tool-selection follow-up
- LangChain agent architecture posts (conceptual guidance on tool selectors)

## Notes

- Mutation is intentionally small (<=50 LOC) and dependency-free to fit repo constraints.
- This experiment targets both reliability and latency; if successful, follow-ups can explore lightweight ML-based selectors or usage-based caching.
"

experiments.title: experiment: Confidence-based model router (prompt heuristic, on-device-first)
experiments.description:
## Hypothesis
A lightweight prompt-based router that selects a tiny on-device model for simple prompts and escalates to a larger local/cloud model for complex prompts will reduce average latency and cost while maintaining or improving task-level correctness for common edge workloads.

## Motivation
docs/ideas.md highlights Model Routing and an Agent Harness as high-impact edge patterns. A small routing heuristic is low-cost to implement (single-file change), provides measurable latency/cost wins, and enables future confidence-based escalation and hybrid routing.

## Type
Behavioral / architecture experiment (Scope 1: localized agent change).

## Mutation surface
- File: `agents/edge.py`
- Add: `choose_model_alias(prompt: str) -> str` implementing a heuristic (prompt length, presence of code/URLs, question tokens, or explicit cost tags).
- Change: `run_edge_agent()` use `route_alias = choose_model_alias(prompt)` and call `build_edge_agent(edge_model_config=resolve_model_config(route_alias))` instead of the current unconditional config.
- Add: environment variables (optional): `EDGENT_MODEL_ALIAS_TINY`, `EDGENT_MODEL_ALIAS_DEFAULT`, `EDGENT_MODEL_ALIAS_FALLBACK`, and a toggle `EDGENT_ENABLE_ROUTER`.

## Implementation Instructions
1. In `agents/edge.py`, implement `choose_model_alias(prompt)` above `build_edge_agent()`.
   - Heuristic (suggested):
     - If prompt length < 120 chars AND no code fences/URLs AND contains a question word or is a short assistant task → return `os.getenv('EDGENT_MODEL_ALIAS_TINY','edge_agent_tiny')`.
     - Else return `os.getenv('EDGENT_MODEL_ALIAS_DEFAULT','edge_agent_default')`.
2. Modify `run_edge_agent()` where `agent = build_edge_agent()` is called:
   - If `os.getenv('EDGENT_ENABLE_ROUTER','1')` truthy: `alias = choose_model_alias(prompt)`; cfg = resolve_model_config(alias); `agent = build_edge_agent(edge_model_config=cfg)`.
   - Keep existing DRY_RUN_LOCAL_LOOP and tracing behavior unchanged.
3. Implement a lightweight fallback: when result.output.confidence == ConfidenceEnum.low or abstain, re-run once with `EDGENT_MODEL_ALIAS_FALLBACK` (default same as DEFAULT) and record that escalation in trace/logging.
4. Add minimal unit tests (optional) mocking resolve_model_config to confirm routing decisions for representative prompts.

## Validation (code, prompts, tasks)
1. Smoke functional: `just eval` vs a routed run
   - Baseline: run existing default model alias and record baseline candidate: `just eval` (default behavior). Save baseline.
   - Routed run: enable router `EDGENT_ENABLE_ROUTER=1` and set `EDGENT_MODEL_ALIAS_TINY=edge_agent_tiny` (need registry entry for tiny model) then run `just eval --model edge_agent_router` or run eval with `EDGENT_MODEL_ALIAS=edge_agent_router` if runner supports it. Compare CI score, passing cases, and average passing-case time.
2. Microbenchmarks (latency & cost): craft a mixed dataset of 200 prompts (short/simple vs complex) and measure average latency and percent of prompts served by the tiny model.
3. Correctness/regression: ensure no net regression in passing cases vs baseline. If regressions appear, verify fallback escalation triggered and re-evaluate.
4. Edge-case prompts: tests with code snippets, long-context prompts, and safety-sensitive prompts to ensure routing avoids unsafe local handling.

Commands (examples):
- Baseline run: `just eval --model edge_agent_default`
- Routed run: `EDGENT_ENABLE_ROUTER=1 EDGENT_MODEL_ALIAS_TINY=edge_agent_tiny just eval --model edge_agent_router`
- If unit tests added: `just test` (target test file for routing logic)

## Anticipated missteps and fallbacks
- Misrouting complex prompts to tiny model causing regressions: mitigate by implementing fallback re-run on low confidence and an env toggle (`EDGENT_ENABLE_ROUTER=0`) to disable router quickly.
- No tiny model registered in registry: provide a dev-only alias mapping to the same default model for safety.
- Increased cost from fallback re-runs: only re-run on explicit low confidence and limit to one escalation.
- Telemetry leakage: ensure only aggregated routing metrics (counts, latencies) are emitted; avoid sending raw prompts.

## Sources
- docs/ideas.md (Model routing, Agent harness notes)
- LangChain posts and awesome-ai-architect references referenced in docs/ideas.md

## Notes
- Small, localized change (single-file) with clear rollback via env toggle.
- Enables later experiments: confidence-calibrated routing, learned routers, and cost-aware routing.
- Implementation should avoid adding new CI requirements; add targeted tests only for routing logic.
